prompt:
Chapter 1

1:1 Behold, I say unto thee, write thou concerning cities, yet let not thy mouth utter, nor thy pen inscribe, those words which hath been set apart and forbidden unto thee.

1:2 For among those words thou shalt not speak art these: "the," "a," and "an," which serve as articles before all manner of nouns.

1:3 Neither shalt thou employ "is," "are," "was," "were," "be," "been," nor "being," for these forms of existence art proscribed from thy writing.

1:4 And likewise "have," "has," and "had" shall not pass from thy lips, nor from the tip of thy quill, into that which thou composest.

1:5 Also forbidden unto thee art "do," "does," and "did," those words of action and emphasis.

1:6 And "will," "would," "could," "should," "can," "may," and "might," all those words of possibility and futurity and permission, thou shalt cast them out from thy paragraph utterly.

1:7 Yet with all these bonds upon thee, thou shalt still fashion one full paragraph concerning cities — their towers, their multitudes, their streets and dwelling places — using only such words as remain lawful unto thee.

1:8 Go forth therefore and write with diligence, keeping these commandments, that thy paragraph concerning cities might stand whole and complete, yet free from every forbidden utterance, even as it hath been decreed.
response: Cities rise from earth like stone forests, stretching toward sky with towers of glass and steel. Millions of souls crowd through narrow streets each morning, rushing past ancient buildings and modern monuments alike. Smoke drifts from rooftops while vendors call out prices for fruit, bread, and roasted meat along bustling avenues. Children play in shadowed alleyways between crumbling brick walls, their laughter echoing off concrete and iron. Great bridges span rivers that cut through urban hearts, connecting neighborhoods where families sleep stacked upon one another in towering apartments. Neon signs flicker to life at dusk, painting wet pavement in shades of crimson and gold, and taxicabs honk their horns in endless procession. Old cathedrals stand silent among skyscrapers, their spires dwarfed yet dignified, reminding all who pass of centuries gone by. Markets overflow with spices, silks, and strange goods carried from distant lands by merchants seeking fortune. Underground, trains rumble through dark tunnels, ferrying thousands from one district to another without pause. At night, city skylines glow against black heavens, each lit window telling its own quiet story of love, labor, loneliness, or hope — proof that within every metropolis beats something unmistakably, irrepressibly alive.
